shortDescription: Traditional Korean medicine (hanbang) in Seoul — acupuncture, Chuna therapy, cupping and custom herbal decoctions for pain and wellness.
quickAnswer: Korean medicine (**hanbang**) is a traditional therapy system using **acupuncture, Chuna manual therapy, cupping, moxibustion and herbal decoctions** to treat pain, musculoskeletal issues and general wellness.
It is **non-surgical and outpatient** — most sessions take 30–60 minutes with **no anaesthesia and no downtime**.
Single treatments are very affordable (acupuncture from roughly **$5–$15**); custom **herbal decoction courses** run higher, often **$200–$500**.
Seoul's **Gangnam**, Myeongdong and Insadong districts have foreigner-friendly Korean medicine clinics with English-speaking staff.

keyFacts.label: Recommended stay in Korea
keyFacts.value: Same-day for a single visit; 1–2 weeks for a treatment course

keyFacts.label: Recovery
keyFacts.value: No downtime — resume normal activity immediately

keyFacts.label: Typical cost in Korea
keyFacts.value: ~$5–$15 per acupuncture session; ~$200–$500 for herbal courses

sections.overview:
Traditional Korean medicine — known locally as **hanbang** (한방) and practised at a **Hanuiwon** (한의원) clinic — is a centuries-old system of care that treats the body as an interconnected whole rather than a set of isolated symptoms.

Its core therapies include:

- **Acupuncture** — fine needles placed at specific points to regulate energy flow and relieve pain
- **Chuna manual therapy** — hands-on spinal and joint adjustment, similar in spirit to chiropractic care
- **Cupping (buhwang)** and **moxibustion** — suction cups and warming herbs to ease tension and improve circulation
- **Herbal medicine** — custom decoctions blended to a patient's needs

Korea has modernised this tradition with licensed practitioners, clinical research and integration alongside conventional care. Practitioners are trained at dedicated universities, and the field sits within the regulated health system rather than on the fringe.

It is most often sought for musculoskeletal pain, stress, fatigue, digestion and general wellness. Because it is fully outpatient, non-surgical and drug-free, many international visitors combine a course of treatment with a normal travel itinerary.
sections.candidacy:
Korean medicine suits a wide range of people because it is gentle and non-invasive. It is commonly chosen by those who:

- Have **musculoskeletal pain** — neck, shoulder, lower back, frozen shoulder or sports strains
- Are recovering from **herniated discs** or postural problems and want conservative, hands-on care
- Experience **chronic fatigue, poor sleep, stress** or digestive complaints
- Prefer a **drug-free, surgery-free** approach or want to complement conventional treatment

A practitioner will assess your overall constitution before recommending a plan — some clinics use **Sasang constitutional medicine**, which classifies people into types to tailor herbs and therapy.

It is not a substitute for emergency or acute surgical care. If you have a serious or rapidly worsening condition, a complex medication list, or are pregnant, share your full history first so the clinician can advise whether hanbang is appropriate and safe for you.

Because treatment is tailored, the practitioner may adjust your plan after the first visit based on how you respond, so it suits people open to a short series of sessions.
sections.procedure_steps:
A typical visit is straightforward and unhurried.

- **Consultation** — the practitioner reviews your symptoms and history, checks your pulse and tongue, and assesses posture or problem areas
- **Diagnosis** — findings guide which therapies are combined for your session
- **Treatment** — depending on the plan this may include acupuncture, cupping, moxibustion or Chuna adjustment

During **acupuncture**, thin needles are inserted and left in place for around 15–20 minutes while you rest. Sensations are usually mild — a brief prick or dull warmth.

**Chuna manual therapy** involves the practitioner gently pulling, pushing or aligning the spine and joints by hand.

If herbal medicine is prescribed, a **custom decoction** is prepared for you to take home over a set course, blended to your symptoms and constitution.

Sessions generally last **30–60 minutes**, depending on how many therapies are combined. Everything is done while you are awake and comfortable, so there is no anaesthesia and no preparation such as fasting.

When the session ends you can dress, leave and resume your day immediately afterwards — there is nothing to recover from before you walk out.
sections.recovery_timeline:
One of the main attractions of Korean medicine is that there is **essentially no recovery period**.

- **Immediately after** — you can walk out and return to normal activity; some people feel relaxed or pleasantly tired
- **First 24 hours** — minor, temporary effects such as small marks from cupping or slight soreness at needle sites may appear and fade quickly
- **Following days** — many feel the benefit build gradually, especially for pain and tension

Because it is non-surgical, there are no stitches, no wound care and no activity restrictions in most cases. Drinking water and resting on the treatment day is usually all that is advised.

For lasting results with chronic complaints, practitioners often recommend a **series of sessions** over one to several weeks rather than a single visit. If you are travelling specifically for a course of care, plan a stay that lets you complete the recommended schedule.
sections.cost_breakdown:
Korean medicine is among the more affordable forms of care in Korea, and prices are transparent.

- A single **acupuncture session** is often only a few dollars to around $15 without local insurance
- **Cupping or moxibustion** add a small amount per session
- **Chuna manual therapy** is modestly priced per visit
- **Custom herbal decoctions** are the larger cost, since they are prepared individually from many ingredients

A first visit that bundles consultation, acupuncture and adjunct therapies commonly totals around **$100–$150**. Herbal courses can range widely, often **$200–$500**, with longer or premium programs costing more.

For international patients, the overall value is strong: care that would be expensive elsewhere is accessible here, and many clinics in tourist districts publish English pricing. Always confirm what is included before you begin so there are no surprises.

When budgeting a trip, remember the largest variable is whether you need a herbal course and how many sessions your plan calls for. A handful of acupuncture visits stays inexpensive, while a multi-week program with custom herbs naturally costs more.

Asking the clinic for a written plan up front makes the total predictable.
sections.why_korea:
Korea offers a rare combination — a **living traditional medicine tradition** delivered with modern standards and oversight.

Korean medicine is a **licensed, regulated profession** here, taught at dedicated universities and integrated into the wider health system rather than sitting on the fringe. The government actively promotes the country as a medical-travel destination, and Korean clinics are known for advanced facilities and competitive pricing.

For visitors, Seoul is especially convenient:

- **Gangnam, Myeongdong, Insadong and Hongdae** have clinics used to international patients
- Many offer **English-speaking staff** or English intake forms
- Treatments such as **Chuna manual therapy** and **Sasang constitutional medicine** are distinctive to the Korean tradition

This blend of authenticity, accessibility and quality is what draws people to experience hanbang at its source rather than at home. Visiting Korea also means access to the full range of therapies under one roof, often within a single clinic, rather than piecing care together from separate providers.

callouts.title: Choose a foreigner-friendly clinic
callouts.text: Clinics in Gangnam, Myeongdong and Insadong are more likely to have English-speaking staff or English intake forms. Confirm language support and total pricing when you book, and ask whether your plan needs several sessions so you can schedule your stay accordingly.

keyTakeaways: Korean medicine (hanbang) is a non-surgical, outpatient tradition using acupuncture, Chuna manual therapy, cupping, moxibustion and herbal decoctions.
Single treatments are inexpensive and there is no anaesthesia and no downtime; herbal courses are the main cost.
Seoul's Gangnam and central tourist districts host foreigner-friendly clinics, and chronic conditions usually benefit from a series of sessions.

faqs.a: Most therapies are gentle. Acupuncture needles are very fine and usually cause only a brief prick or mild dull sensation, and Chuna manual therapy uses controlled hands-on adjustments. Some people feel minor soreness or see temporary cupping marks afterward, both of which fade quickly.

faqs.q: How much does Korean medicine cost in Korea?
faqs.a: A single acupuncture session is often only a few dollars up to around $15 without local insurance. A first visit bundling consultation and several therapies commonly totals about $100–$150, while custom herbal decoction courses typically run $200–$500 depending on ingredients and duration.

faqs.q: Do I need to stay overnight or take recovery time?
faqs.a: No. Korean medicine is fully outpatient with no anaesthesia and essentially no downtime. You can return to normal activity immediately after a session. For chronic conditions, practitioners often recommend a series of visits over one to several weeks.

faqs.q: Can foreigners get Korean medicine treatment in Seoul easily?
faqs.a: Yes. Clinics in tourist and business districts such as Gangnam, Myeongdong and Insadong are accustomed to international patients and frequently offer English-speaking staff or English intake forms. It helps to confirm language support and pricing when booking.

faqs.q: What conditions is Korean medicine used for?
faqs.a: It is most often used for musculoskeletal pain such as neck, shoulder and lower-back pain, frozen shoulder, herniated discs and sports strains, as well as stress, fatigue, sleep and digestive complaints. It is a conservative, drug-free option and not a replacement for emergency or surgical care.

references.label: An introduction to Chuna manual medicine in Korea (NCBI/PMC)
